title: Introduction to UI/UX Design for Informatics Students
excerpt: Why Informatics students should master the basics of User Interface and User Experience design, and how to start using Figma for layout design.
content:
As Informatics Engineering students, we are often trained to focus heavily on the logic, algorithm, and back-end structure of software applications. However, a system is only as good as its usability. This is where UI/UX design becomes essential.

### Why UI/UX Matters
UI (User Interface) refers to the visual look and feel of an application—its buttons, text, images, text fields, and transitions. UX (User Experience) represents the overall feeling that a user gets when interacting with the application. If the interface is confusing or sluggish, the user will quickly abandon the software, regardless of how efficient the underlying code is.

### 3 Core Pillars for Beginners
1. **Consistency**: Use a cohesive color palette and a consistent typographic hierarchy throughout your design. This makes the interface feel unified.
2. **Visual Hierarchy**: Guide the user's eye to the most important elements (like Call-To-Action buttons) using size, weight, and contrast.
3. **Feedback**: Always provide visual feedback for user actions (e.g., hover states on buttons, loading indicators, and toast messages).

### Figma as Your Starting Tool
Figma is an excellent browser-based design tool. To start:
- Create a simple frame matching your target screen size.
- Lay out basic shapes (rectangles, text elements) to represent components.
- Use components and autolayouts to keep the alignment perfect. 

By taking a user-centered design approach, you will build applications that are not only high-performing but also highly engaging.

title: Modern Layouts and Responsive Web Design in 2026
excerpt: An in-depth look into modern responsive CSS layout strategies like CSS Grid, Flexbox, and fluid typography without relying heavily on frameworks.
content:
Designing websites that look pristine on a 4K desktop monitor as well as a compact mobile screen is a standard requirement. In 2026, responsive design is moving beyond simple media queries toward fluid, container-relative layouts.

### Moving Beyond Standard Media Queries
While `@media` queries are still useful, they can lead to abrupt screen jumps. Modern layouts leverage built-in responsive sizing functions:
- **`clamp()`**: Fluid typography and spacing using `font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 3rem)`. This scales typography smoothly from a minimum to a maximum bound based on viewport width.
- **CSS Grid Auto-Fit**: Construct flexible grids that automatically warp to wrap columns without writing media queries: `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr))`;
- **Aspect Ratio**: The CSS property `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9` prevents layout shifts when images or videos load.

### Emphasizing Glassmorphism and Micro-Animations
Premium layouts in 2026 use glassmorphism elements (`backdrop-filter: blur(12px)`) combined with soft radial gradients as background highlights. Subtle transitions on hover (like `transform: translateY(-4px)`) provide tactile feedback that increases visitor satisfaction.

By focusing on standard web technologies like native CSS, you reduce bundle size and deliver lightning-fast loading speeds on mobile networks.
